executive_summary: WIN-065 contains a critical factual error: the summary claims a +0.27° excess at 35.9°N latitude, but the dome's actual PRED-NEW-001 prediction claims 3-7° excess *above 55°N* (Oslo +4.5°, Helsinki +5.5°, Edinburgh +1.4°). These are incompatible claims. The +0.27° figure is statistically insignificant (within Polaris's 0.74° polar offset plus refraction and measurement error). The dome's own site contradicts WIN-065 by documenting Polaris altitude matching WGS84 latitude (the globe prediction) as FALSIFIED (2026-03-15). Furthermore, WIN-065 depends entirely on aetheric refraction (an unmeasurable, unfalsifiable correction function) and lacks any published methodology. The verdict is sound, but the claim description is inaccurate.

dome_defense_attempt_1.title: The +0.27° excess is real and measurable
dome_defense_attempt_1.argument: The dome could argue that 0.27° is a genuine systematic offset distinct from Polaris's polar offset and refraction. If replicated measurements by multiple observers at 35.9°N show consistent +0.27° deviation, this would be noteworthy. The error budget analysis (Polaris offset ~0.74°, refraction ~0.02-0.03°, instrument uncertainty ±0.2-0.5°) might underestimate the actual combined uncertainty.
dome_defense_attempt_1.our_counterargument: Three critical flaws: (1) The dome provides NO published methodology — no instrument specification, number of measurements, time of night, atmospheric conditions, or how the 'predicted' value was calculated. A single unreplicated measurement within the noise floor is not confirmation. (2) The error budget is *conservative*. Polaris's offset alone is ±0.74°, atmospheric refraction varies ±0.02-0.03° depending on elevation angle and barometric pressure. For a 35° elevation angle, combined uncertainty easily reaches ±0.3-0.5°. (3) Most crucially: this contradicts the dome's own PRED-NEW-001 prediction, which makes a *much larger* claim: 3-7° excess above 55°N. If the dome's own prediction at higher latitudes is wildly off, how does it salvage the 35.9°N measurement?

dome_defense_attempt_2.title: The 35.9°N and 55°N claims are both correct — the dome's H(r) function predicts different excesses at different latitudes
dome_defense_attempt_2.argument: The dome's H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) km function predicts that Polaris elevation varies nonlinearly with latitude. At 35.9°N (r≈6500 km), the projection yields +0.27°. At 55°N (r≈2753 km), it yields larger excess like +4.5°. The two claims are not contradictory but complementary — latitude-dependent predictions from one model.
dome_defense_attempt_2.our_counterargument: This defense fails because: (1) The coordinate system is backwards. In dome geometry, lower-latitude cities are at *larger* radii (further from the north pole axis). Oslo at 59.91°N has r=2753 km; a city at 35.9°N would have r≈6500 km (further out). If the excess decreases with increasing r (as the math shows), the 35.9°N point should show *less* excess than the 55°N points, not vice versa. The dome predicts +4.5° at 55°N; it should predict even *larger* at 35.9°N if anything, yet reports only +0.27°. This is backwards. (2) The dome coordinates file explicitly lists: 'Chapel Hill NC: Polaris 36.18, Noon UTC 17.25, r=6495, theta=+78.8' — a location very close to 35.9°N. But no Polaris excess measurement is documented for Chapel Hill. (3) Most fatally: the dome's own site marks the broader claim ('Polaris elevation matches latitude at all locations') as FALSIFIED. If that broader claim is falsified, how can specific latitude-dependent sub-predictions be salvaged? The model cannot both claim falsification of its main Polaris prediction and confirmation of a narrower subset of the same data.

dome_defense_attempt_3.title: The standard model cannot explain the +0.27° excess — this is genuinely anomalous
dome_defense_attempt_3.argument: On a spherical Earth, Polaris altitude equals observer latitude (to arcsecond precision after pole-star corrections). A systematic +0.27° excess cannot be explained by refraction (too large and in wrong direction at 35°N). This anomaly supports the dome's geometry where Polaris is a fixed point displaced from the geographic pole by the dome's projection.
dome_defense_attempt_3.our_counterargument: This confuses ignorance with evidence. A +0.27° effect that is (1) unreplicated, (2) unspecified in methodology, (3) within the measurement error budget (±0.3-0.5° combined), and (4) contradicted by centuries of astronomical navigation data is not an 'anomaly' — it is noise or artifact. Every celestial navigation authority — USNO, Hydrographic Office, astronomical handbooks — has published pole-star correction tables that, when applied, reduce Polaris altitude error to arcseconds. These tables have been validated on tens of thousands of observations over centuries. A single unreplicated measurement of +0.27° has zero weight against that corpus. Furthermore, refraction at 35° elevation is ~0.02° (small) but not zero, and Polaris's 0.74° polar offset produces a ±0.74° envelope of variation over the night — any sub-degree measurement falls within this envelope. The dome's claim requires (a) publishing methodology, (b) replicating the measurement at multiple sites and observers, (c) demonstrating the effect is distinct from refraction and Polaris's offset, and (d) explaining why it vanishes at higher latitudes (contradicting PRED-NEW-001's 3-7° claims). None of this has been done.

dome_defense_attempt_4.title: Aetheric refraction is real and measurable through Polaris observations
dome_defense_attempt_4.argument: The +0.27° excess is precisely what we'd expect from aetheric refraction in the dome model. At 35.9°N (r≈6500 km), the refractive index n(r) = 1 + 0.20·(r/20015)^2 ≈ 1.0065. This refracts light downward by ~0.27°. The dome's refraction hypothesis is testable and coherent.
dome_defense_attempt_4.our_counterargument: This is the core unfalsifiability trap. (1) The refractive index formula is never published on the dome's website — it is inferred *post hoc* from the claimed measurement. The formula n(r) = 1 + 0.20·(r/20015)^2 is itself derived from 'whatever adjustment makes the data fit,' not from independent physical principles. (2) If refraction can explain the +0.27° at 35.9°N, it should also explain the claimed 3-7° excess at 55°N. Let's check: at r=2753 km (Oslo, 59.91°N), n ≈ 1 + 0.20·(2753/20015)^2 ≈ 1.00075, which would refract light by only ~0.04° — far short of the claimed +4.5°. The formula breaks completely. (3) The dome model's list of cities includes many above 55°N (Reykjavik 64.10°N, Anchorage 61.20°N, Moscow 55.75°N) for which no Polaris excess measurements are published, despite PRED-NEW-001 registering predictions for Oslo and Helsinki. Why are those cities not tested? (4) Most fundamentally: aetheric refraction has no independent measurement. The only evidence for it is the data it was invented to explain. You cannot use 'aetheric refraction explains this' as evidence for aetheric refraction — that is circular reasoning. The model is unfalsifiable because any contradiction with observations is blamed on unspecified refraction effects.

code_analysis_validation.status: PENDING - tags should be assigned upon full review, but preliminary assessment:
code_analysis_validation.monitoring_notes: WIN-065 does not appear in any monitoring script. There is no automated test to replicate the +0.27° measurement or validate the broader PRED-NEW-001 claims. The prediction remains 'PENDING' on the dome's tracking page with no evidence collection visible.
code_analysis_validation.relabels_standard_notes: The entire claim depends on redefining celestial navigation. Standard astronomy defines 'observer latitude = Polaris altitude (to arcsecond precision after pole-star corrections).' The dome claims systematic deviation requires its unique geometry and aetheric refraction. In reality, the dome is relabeling normal celestial navigation as evidence for its model. The refraction effect, if real, would contradict the standard model — but no published, replicated, methodology-transparent measurement exists.
code_analysis_validation.post_hoc_notes: PRED-NEW-001 was registered 2026-03-12, but the dome's coordinates file dates from earlier versions (V12, mentioned in the prediction text). The Polaris observations may have been known beforehand and incorporated into the prediction as a 'fit.' The +0.27° figure is presented as a specific measurement outcome, not as a prediction derived from dome geometry before observation.
code_analysis_validation.derives_from_dome_notes: WIN-065 does not derive from dome geometry. The dome's geometry (H(r) function) cannot independently predict what Polaris altitude *should* be without invoking an undefined aetheric refraction function. The H(r) curve describes the firmament height over the disc, not the angular position of a fixed point (Polaris). To claim Polaris altitude from H(r) requires mapping observer latitude → radial distance r, then applying H(r), then applying refraction — three steps, the last of which is undefined.

simbad_reference.note: Simbad (SIMBAD Astronomical Database) does maintain Polaris coordinates. Current declination is approximately +89.26°, and Polaris is indeed ~0.74° from true celestial pole at epoch 2026.

hemisphere_navigation.cited_as: Centuries of celestial navigation confirm Polaris altitude = latitude (to arcsecond precision)
hemisphere_navigation.note: This is a fundamental principle in navigation. USNO, Hydrographic Office, and numerous sailing-instruction handbooks document pole-star corrections. The claim that Polaris altitude matches latitude is standard and well-documented.

kill_shot_test_2.cited_as: See Section 4.3 (Kill-Shot Test 2) for full analysis
kill_shot_test_2.note: The Kill-Shot Test 2 analysis in generate-html.js provides comprehensive critique of the +0.27° claim, documenting measurement uncertainty, lack of methodology, and contradiction with the dome's own falsification of the broader Polaris prediction.

contradiction_1.issue: WIN-065 claims +0.27° excess at 35.9°N; PRED-NEW-001 claims 3-7° excess above 55°N
contradiction_1.impact: These are incompatible assertions from the same model. At higher latitudes (closer to dome apex), the dome predicts *larger* excess due to stronger aetheric effects. Yet WIN-065's +0.27° is reported from a *lower* latitude. This violates the dome's own geometric principle.

contradiction_2.issue: Dome's own site marks 'Polaris elevation matches WGS84 latitude' as FALSIFIED (2026-03-15), yet WIN-065 claims confirmation of Polaris excess
contradiction_2.impact: These are opposite verdicts on the same phenomenon. Either Polaris altitude matches latitude (globe prediction, confirmed) or it shows systematic excess (dome prediction, claimed). The dome cannot claim both falsification and confirmation of the same prediction.

contradiction_3.issue: Aetheric refraction formula n(r) = 1 + 0.20·(r/20015)² predicts only ~0.04° at Oslo (r=2753 km), not the claimed +4.5°
contradiction_3.impact: The proposed refraction mechanism cannot produce the claimed effect at the predicted latitudes. The formula fails its own test.

unfalsifiability_analysis.mechanism_of_unfalsifiability: WIN-065 depends entirely on aetheric refraction, which has (1) no independent measurement, (2) no published functional form, (3) free parameters that adjust to fit data, (4) no physical derivation. Any discrepancy between observation and dome geometry is explained by invoking refraction—which is not a theory but a placeholder for 'whatever correction is needed.'
unfalsifiability_analysis.why_this_matters: A scientific model must make predictions *before* observations. If every observation that contradicts the model is explained by invoking a new correction function (refraction), the model becomes unfalsifiable. WIN-065 is one of at least six WINs (also WIN-016, 017, 026, 033, 056) that rely on this mechanism. Together, they render the dome's optical predictions immune to empirical test.
unfalsifiability_analysis.test_for_unfalsifiability: If we observe Polaris at 35.9°N and find the altitude is *not* 0.27° higher than latitude, the dome can claim refraction is weaker than expected. If we observe +0.27°, it claims refraction confirmed it. If we observe +1.0°, it claims refraction is stronger. There is no observation that falsifies the model because refraction can adjust. This is not prediction; it is post-hoc narrative.

verdict_assessment.assessment: The verdict is sound, but the reasoning could be sharpened. The main evidence is that the +0.27° figure is within the error budget (Polaris offset ±0.74° + refraction ±0.02-0.03° + measurement uncertainty ±0.2-0.5° = combined ±0.3-0.5°). Crucially, the dome provides no published methodology to replicate the measurement. The secondary evidence — that the dome's site contradicts this by documenting Polaris-latitude matching as confirmed — is even stronger: it shows the dome cannot coherently claim both outcomes.
verdict_assessment.recommendation: Maintain 'Refuted by Data' verdict. The +0.27° excess is not statistically significant given the error budget, is unreplicated, and lacks methodology. Additionally note that the dome's broader Polaris prediction is contradicted by its own site.

claim_accuracy_error.description: WIN-065 misrepresents the dome's actual prediction. The wins.json entry claims +0.27° at 35.9°N, but PRED-NEW-001 claims 3-7° above 55°N. This is a factual error in how the WIN is summarized.

aetheric_refraction_unfalsifiability.description: WIN-065 depends on an undefined, unmeasurable correction function. This is a core epistemological flaw that applies to at least 6 WINs.

internal_dome_contradiction.description: The dome simultaneously claims Polaris-latitude matching (confirmed) and Polaris-excess (WIN-065 confirmed). These cannot both be true.

lack_of_methodology.description: No instrument, measurement procedure, data collection protocol, or error analysis is published. The claim is unreplicable.

suggested_improvements_to_review.1_claim_correction: Update wins.json to clarify: 'PRED-NEW-001 claims Polaris elevation 3-7° higher than WGS84 latitude at cities above 55°N (Oslo +4.5°, Helsinki +5.5°, Edinburgh +1.4°). The +0.27° figure at 35.9°N does not appear in official dome predictions and contradicts the model's own latitude-dependence claims.'
suggested_improvements_to_review.2_internal_contradiction_flag: Document the contradiction: 'Dome site (2026-03-15) marks "Polaris altitude matches latitude" as FALSIFIED, yet WIN-065 claims confirmation of Polaris excess. These are incompatible.'
suggested_improvements_to_review.3_methodology_requirement: Expand finding to state: 'No published methodology. Lacks: instrument specification, sample size, observation time-of-night, atmospheric conditions, calculation of predicted value, error analysis, or replication by independent observers.'
suggested_improvements_to_review.4_refraction_unfalsifiability: Cross-reference Section 4.9 (Aetheric Refraction) and note that WIN-065 is one of six WINs depending on an unfalsifiable correction mechanism.

holes_found.title: Claim mismatch between wins.json and PRED-NEW-001
holes_found.description: wins.json says +0.27° at 35.9°N; PRED-NEW-001 says 3-7° above 55°N. These are incompatible. Either the summary is wrong, or the prediction is wrong.
holes_found.likelihood_opponent_finds_this: VERY HIGH - any close reading of the dome's own prediction file will reveal this discrepancy.

holes_found.title: No published measurement methodology
holes_found.description: The dome does not specify what instrument was used, how many measurements, at what time(s), under what conditions, how the prediction was calculated, or what the error budget is. A single unreplicated measurement is not confirmation.
holes_found.likelihood_opponent_finds_this: VERY HIGH - any scientist will immediately ask for methodology.

holes_found.title: Contradiction with dome's own falsification of broader Polaris prediction
holes_found.description: Dome's site (2026-03-15) marks 'Polaris altitude matches latitude' as FALSIFIED. This is the broader prediction of which WIN-065 is a special case. The dome cannot claim both falsification and confirmation of related predictions.
holes_found.likelihood_opponent_finds_this: VERY HIGH - anyone reading the dome's own site will see this.

holes_found.title: Aetheric refraction formula doesn't match claimed PRED-NEW-001 values
holes_found.description: If n(r) = 1 + 0.20·(r/20015)² predicts refraction at different latitudes, it produces ~0.04° at Oslo (59.91°N, r=2753 km), not the claimed +4.5°. The formula fails on the dome's own test case.
holes_found.likelihood_opponent_finds_this: HIGH - straightforward calculation from dome's own published parameters.

holes_found.title: PRED-NEW-001 registered with expected outcome (no blind prediction)
holes_found.description: PRED-NEW-001 was registered 2026-03-12 after dome site changes and development work. It may reflect known measurements rather than prospective prediction.
holes_found.likelihood_opponent_finds_this: MEDIUM - requires checking version history and timestamps.

holes_found.title: Polaris offset and refraction error budget fully explains +0.27°
holes_found.description: Combined error from Polaris's 0.74° polar offset, ~0.02-0.03° refraction, and ±0.2-0.5° measurement uncertainty is ±0.3-0.5°. The +0.27° figure falls entirely within this band and is not statistically significant.
holes_found.likelihood_opponent_finds_this: MEDIUM - requires quantitative error analysis, but accessible.

final_assessment.reason: WIN-065's verdict (Refuted by Data) is correct. However, the claim description in wins.json is factually inaccurate (mismatches PRED-NEW-001), and the supporting evidence could be strengthened by documenting the dome's own internal contradictions and the lack of published methodology. The fundamental issue remains: the claim is statistically insignificant (within error), unreplicated, and depends on unfalsifiable aetheric refraction.
